context: The top of both boards. You can see the Wolfson chip to the left of the flash. title: iPod Touch 1st Generation Teardown
context: Here's a comparison of Touch and iPhone processor markings. Touch: 339S0029ARM 8900B iPhone: 339S0030ARM 8900B (very similar) Touch: 0731 (manufactured week 31 of 2007) iPhone: 0719 (week 19 of 2007). Touch: NOKCYY2 iPhone: NOD4BZ02. The last number is probably a lot id, which explains why they are so different. What does this mean? The iPod Touch and iPhone processors are very, very similar.

title: iPod Touch 1st Generation Teardown
context: From what we are told, a Bluetooth antenna (if it existed) could be integrated into a PCB like this one, making identification difficult. The 'RF1UB' marking on the back of the board indicates that it's been designed for RF, and probably tested at frequency. So, while we don't see evidence of a Bluetooth antenna, we cannot rule out its existence.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: The iPhone 4 handset has Knowles S1950 and Infineon 1014 microphones (pictured on the left), while the earbuds contain a Knowles S199 mic.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: The iPhone 4 handset has Knowles S1950 and Infineon 1014 microphones (pictured on the left), while the earbuds contain a Knowles S199 mic.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: The first of the two microphones inside the iPhone (the one that you speak into) is the Knowles S1950.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: The Knowles S1950, like the other microphones in the iPhone 4, is comprised of two main parts: the MEMS and the ASIC. You can think of it this way: the MEMS does all the work, and the ASIC does all the thinking. Together, they make an awesome microphone.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: Looking at the S1950 MEMS die gives you an idea of how it functions. Like a microscopic vesion of a condenser microphone, the microphone itself has a simple design, comprising of two parallel polysilicon plates (very thin plates made of multiple small silicon crystals) that act as plates in a capacitor.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: The S1950 ASIC die interprets the analog signals given off by the MEMS die. This portion of the microphone decodes and process the analog signal sent to it from the MEMS and sends the result to the iPhone 4's processor.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown
context: The second design win for Knowles is the S199 microphone located in the headset that comes with the iPhone 4.

title: iPhone 4 Microphone Teardown

title: RCA Studio II Teardown
context: A meager five screws are all that hold the two halves of the Studio II together. That's 500% more screws than in the guide 3531 Odyssey 100, but half the screws required to open the top cover on a guide 3224 PS3 Slim.

title: RCA Studio II Teardown
context: At the heart of the Studio II lies an RCA CDP1802 microprocessor, running at a scorching 1.78 MHz. Coupled with 2K ROM, 512 bytes RAM, and a 64 x 32 pixel monochrome graphics chip, the Studio II was underwhelming even back in 1977. To put things into perspective, the TI-83 calculator (introduced in 1996) operates at 6 MHz and has 32 KB of RAM.

title: RCA Studio II Teardown
context: At the heart of the Studio II lies an RCA CDP1802 microprocessor, running at a scorching 1.78 MHz. Coupled with 2K ROM, 512 bytes RAM, and a 64 x 32 pixel monochrome graphics chip, the Studio II was underwhelming even back in 1977. To put things into perspective, the TI-83 calculator (introduced in 1996) operates at 6 MHz and has 32 KB of RAM.

title: RCA Studio II Teardown
context: The RCA CDP1802 was a bit of an unusual chip for its day. A version of the 1802 was manufactured by depositing a thin film of silicon on a sapphire wafer. The extremely low electrical conductivity of the sapphire wafer prevented any stray electrical current, caused by radiation bombardment, from spreading to (and possibly damaging) nearby transistors on the chip.

context: CDMA, or Code-Division Multiple Access, is actually just a channel access method standard. When most people (including us) say CDMA, they usually mean CDMA2000, which is a family of 3G communication standards that run on top of the CDMA mechanism of utilizing airwaves. Confused? Unless you're an RF engineer, the distinction probably doesn't matter to you.

title: iPhone 5 Teardown
context: We can now confirm that the Qualcomm MDM9615M is the 4G LTE modem powering this new feature.

title: iPhone 5 Teardown
context: The Qualcomm MDM9615M is a 28 nm LTE (FDD and TDD), HSPA+, EV-DO Rev B, TD-SCDMA modem.

title: iPhone 5 Teardown
context: The MDM9615 allows for multi-spectrums, multi-mode LTE support. It is responsible for transmitting simultaneous voice and data transfer on LTE (provided the carrier has the infrastructure to allow simultaneous voice and data transfer.)

title: iPhone 5 Teardown
context: We also find the Qualcomm RTR8600 multi-band/mode RF transceiver. The RTR8600 is paired alongside the MDM9615 to support various bands, including 5 UMTS bands, and over 5 LTE and 4 EDGE bands.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: We wanted to see how the PS3 Super Slim stacks up to the standard slim model, the console it replaces.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: The door is thin, cheap-feeling plastic, and means this PS3 now requires overhead clearance to change the disc. No more cramming the PS3 into a short shelf.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: In addition to the immediate benefit to Sony's margins, lowering production costs also future-proofs the PS3. When the Playstation 4 is finally released, it's almost a certainty that the PlayStation 3 Super Slim will still be sold alongside the new console, but at a much lower price point.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: The PS3 Super Slim's hard drive bay cover pops right off and reveals a bright blue Phillips screw.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: After removing said screw, the Hitachi TravelStar Z5K320 2.5" SATA II 3.0Gbps 250 GB 5400 RPM hard drive comes right out.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: And here we go! What wonders and surprises will we find inside the PS3 Super Slim? The only way to find out is by moving the top cover out of the way.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: The laser lens is labeled as KEM-850 PHA 0672 23020A.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: We read some conflicting reports about the noise level from the PS3 Super Slim's disc drive and were sure to include a look at Sony's noise reduction efforts:

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: In a similar fashion to the PS3 Slim, the power supply is designed for inputs ranging from 100V to 240V AC, making it usable across the globe.

title: PlayStation 3 Super Slim Teardown
context: It seems the overall power consumption has been lowered as well. While the PS3 Slim was rated at 12 V and 18 A DC, the Super Slim is rated at 12 V and 13 A DC.

title: Oculus Rift CV1 Teardown
context: Where prior Oculus Rift kits shipped with interchangeable circular lenses (left), the CV1 uses a single set of non-removable, asymmetric lenses (right).

title: Oculus Rift CV1 Teardown
context: A closeup of the CV1 lens reveals these concentric rings, a telltale sign of Fresnel lenses. These cleverly manufactured lenses do the same job as the thick, bulging, curved plastic lenses seen in prior Rifts—using thin arrays of concentric prisms that weigh far less.

title: Oculus Rift CV1 Teardown
context: The use of dual displays was a major advance between Rift DK2 and the Crescent Bay prototype. By allowing each lens + display assembly to move as a unit, the Rift provides enough adjustment to accommodate the 5th-95th percentile of IPD—while keeping other key optical properties intact.

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: What exactly puts the "e" in S10e, and keeps it out of the S10? Let's see if the specs tell us anything:

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: Super AMOLED Infinity-O displays—5.8" (2280 × 1080) on the S10e and 6.1" (3040 × 1440) on the S10

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: 10-megapixel selfie camera and a rear-facing camera, with one dual-aperture 12 MP wide-angle module and one 16 MP ultra wide module—plus, the S10 gets one additional 12 MP telephoto module

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: A conventional fingerprint sensor in the S10e's side button, vs. the new ultrasonic fingerprint sensor hidden under the S10's display

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: These phones' monolithic facades don't reveal much right off the bat, though we note the curved display edges on the S10 and S10+.

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: From the back we spot two different camera lineups: all of the phones sport wide-angle and ultra-wide cameras, but the S10 and S10+ get bonus telephoto cameras.

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: A dense ceramic back cover makes the S10+ far more opaque to X-rays than its glass-backed companions. In other words, it's dark.

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: The S10 plays things a little closer to the vest, with a fingerprint sensor you can't even see ... without a teardown, that is.

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: Despite their subtle differences, the S10 and S10e both agree that headphone jacks are still pretty cool. They've also got matching USB-C, mic, and speaker ports.

title: Samsung Galaxy S10 and S10e Teardown
context: The S10's array (left) gets one more camera than the S10e—a 12 MP, ƒ/2.4 telephoto with OIS—and sticks it on the same connector as the standard wide-angle camera.

title: Samsung Galaxy A51 Teardown
context: The back cover shines like glass, but it's a ruse! It's plastic—perhaps a cost-saving measure? Whatever the reason, we're happy to see it. This plastic makes the A51 less shatter-prone than the glass-backed Galaxy S phones.

title: Samsung Galaxy A51 Teardown
context: Samsung sends the A51 into the selfie-race with a 32 MP ƒ2.2 front camera. That’s a 7 MP increase from its predecessor—quite instagrammable.

title: Samsung Galaxy A51 Teardown
context: Just like its predecessor, the A51 also features an optical in-display fingerprint scanner which is placed near the daughterboard.

title: Samsung Galaxy A51 Teardown
context: That said, this configuration is better than the single-board Galaxy S10! At least this tiny daughterboard can be swapped out with relative ease should one or both ports fail on you.

title: Samsung Galaxy A51 Teardown
context: Although the A51 is a mid-range smartphone, its camera setup can supposedly keep up with the more expensive ones. Samsung claims that its four cameras ensure perfect everyday photography.

title: Samsung Galaxy A51 Teardown
context: Now that the battery is out, we can compare it to its competitors! Fully topped off, this 3.85 V, 4000 mAh cell will deliver up to 15.4 Wh of power. That’s more than the iPhone 11 Pro Max with 15.04 Wh and only slightly less than the 15.79 Wh battery in the S10+.

context: Like all Samsung phones, the A51's screen is strongly glued to the frame. There's a reason that Samsung sells screen replacements pre-adhered to brand new frames, and this is it!

title: Samsung Galaxy Note 20 and Note 20 Ultra Teardown
context: Come to think of it, we saw something similar in the S20 Ultra.

title: Samsung Galaxy Note 20 and Note 20 Ultra Teardown
context: Each phone gets its earpiece speaker mounted to the upper antenna assembly, which contains a dedicated speaker enclosure—that's a first! The speaker is the rectangular bit framed with a bright red gasket. It still fires through a channel in the midframe, much like it did on the Note10 and S20—but the new larger enclosures ought to boost audio quality a bit.

title: Samsung Galaxy Note 20 and Note 20 Ultra Teardown
context: The Ultra inherits the super-cool optical Space Zoom module from the S20 Ultra, but with a smaller 12 MP sensor behind it. (Click the link for a detailed take-apart of the periscope mechanism.) The regular Note 20 doesn't "space zoom," instead relying on a dense 64 MP sensor and some cropping magic.

title: Samsung Galaxy Note 20 and Note 20 Ultra Teardown
context: The situation reverses for the wide-angle module, where the Ultra gets a gigantic 108 MP sensor—which, like the S20 Ultra, usually bins down to 12 MP. Meanwhile, the regular Note packs a more modest 12 MP sensor here.

title: Samsung Galaxy Note 20 and Note 20 Ultra Teardown
context: Neither Note gets the S20 Ultra's 40 MP front-facing camera—instead, both pack 10 MP selfie sensors, which appear to come bundled with some unfortunate smoothing effects.

title: PlayStation 5 Teardown
context: We've played a bit of Spider-Man, caught up on sleep, and now it's time to dismantle this PS5. The last fully-fledged, whole-number PlayStation launch was way back in November, 2013, so we're pretty excited. Here's what our gently-used system is packing:

title: PlayStation 5 Teardown
context: You may have heard the PS5 is big—but thankfully, it still fits in Creative Electron's X-ray cabinet. Check out the huge fan, and all the heat pipes overlapping like a freeway interchange.

title: PlayStation 5 Teardown
context: To keep this curvy guy stable, Sony includes a thoughtfully designed stand. With just a bit of fiddling, it allows the PS5 to either pose vertically or lie gracefully on its side.

title: PlayStation 5 Teardown
context: Around back, the PS5 shows off its ports: two USB-A 3.0, LAN, HDMI 2.1, and two-pin power. Add those to the duo of USB ports on the front (one Type A, one Type C), and you've got a reasonably well-connected console.

title: PlayStation 5 Teardown
context: But to go any farther, we need a T8 Torx Security driver. (Why on earth does your PS5's fan need security screws?)

title: PlayStation 5 Teardown
context: Just like the Xbox Series X, our test results indicate that the PS5's optical drive is paired to its motherboard. A replacement drive simply won't read discs.

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: The spec sheet for this phone looks very ultra—but is it more ultra than last year's model? Or is it basically an S20 Ultra in a mask? Judge for yourself:

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: If you read our S20 Ultra teardown and think you're seeing double, you're not alone. But let's dig a little deeper to see if more than just the numbers line up.

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: Camera bumps may be here to stay, but expandable storage has gone the way of the dodo—the microSD tray is missing in the S21 series.

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: The guts seem a slight iteration over last year's S20 Ultra, with minor changes to the wireless charging coil and upper antenna assemblies.

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: The earpiece speaker comes out with the antenna assembly this go-round, which is an updated design we first saw in the Note 20—as opposed to being separately adhered to the frame in prior phones like the S20 Ultra.

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: After we delicately yank the motherboard out, we make a beeline for the camera assemblies, where we're greeted by a familiar friend from the S20 Ultra, the 12 MP ultra-wide.

title: Samsung Galaxy S21 Ultra Teardown
context: One big change to the camera array: The DepthVision camera from the S20+ and Ultra has packed up and moved out, making room for a 10 MP ƒ/2.4 70 mm telephoto camera as the periscope's new roommate.

title: iMac M1 24" Teardown
context: Best guess: The new iMac design was too thin for a CR2032 on the main board. That's right—they couldn't squeeze in the battery from an AirTag. So Apple instead used two half-height CR2016s, and squirreled them away over here for clearance.

context: The PS VR2 still relies on a rubber light shield block light, which means that we get the same washable surface.

context: Overall, the new design of the PS VR2 is a definite improvement over the original, and Sony has improved many of the aspects that made the first PS VR headset so difficult to repair.
